coredata.dc:title: Facile and fast synthesis of N, S-codoped graphene quantum dots for durable luminescent labels: Application and validation through artificial intelligence
coredata.dc:creator: Lighvan, Vahid Ashrafi
Arsalani, Nasser
coredata.dc:description: This study presents the synthesis of blue-emissive nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDblue) via a rapid microwave-assisted pyrolysis method. To overcome the challenge of aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), which limits the application of quantum dots in solid-state devices, we propose embedding the N, S-GQDblue into polymer matrices. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) confirmed the presence of amine active sites, enabling crosslinking with epoxy monomers. Incorporating N, S-GQDblue into an epoxy matrix effectively mitigated ACQ effects while maintaining photoluminescence (PL) intensity, with over 86 % of PL retained after two months of storage. This study demonstrates potential applications in anti-forgery measures through stealth patterning and printing. Additionally, a Python-based image processing script was developed to authenticate printed patterns, achieving 100 % structural similarity and verifying emission wavelength accuracy. These findings advance the development of luminescent labels as foundational security elements, with promising applications in anti-counterfeiting, security devices, and optical sensing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N
S-GQDblue
Epoxy
Luminescent label
Aggregation- caused quenching
Artificial intelligence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of pristine epoxy resins and N, S-GQDblue@ epoxy nanocomposite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the synthesis route of N, S-GQDblue.

a) TEM image of N, S-GQDblue. b) Size distribution histogram of N, S-GQDblue. c, d) the corresponding HRTEM images of (a). e) the corresponding FFT image of (d). f) the corresponding SAED image of (a).

a) XP survey spectra of N, S-GQDblue, b-d) high-resolution b) C 1s, c) N 1s, and d) S 2p spectra of the N, S-GQDblue, Deconvoluted components are presented in the high-resolution spectra, inset of a) Relative contents of C, N, O, and S elements, inset of (b–d) Relative contents of functional groups.
The participation of N, S-GQDblue in the curing of epoxy monomers.
FT-IR spectra of the N, S-GQDblue, Epoxy resin, and N, S-GQDblue@ epoxy nanocomposite.

a) UV–visible absorption (black line) and PL emission (blue line) spectra of N, S-GQDblue, b) PL emission in various excitation-wavelengths. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
PL retention against storage time: N, S-GQDblue@ epoxy nanocomposite versus aqueous solution N, S-GQDblue.
The pattern stamped on leather under a (left) room light and (right) UV lamp (362 nm).
Digital processing steps for the luminescent pattern on paper, alongside the reference image.
Comparison between the present study and a comparable study in the field of luminescent labels.

row.entry: Carbon dots for epoxy curing: Anti-forgery patterns with long-term luminescent stability, Chem. Eng. J. 405 (2021) 126988 [38].

head.ce:title: Facile and fast synthesis of N, S-codoped graphene quantum dots for durable luminescent labels: Application and validation through artificial intelligence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Polymer, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, 29-Bahman Blvd, Tabriz, East-Azarbiajan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research laboratory of Polymer, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, 29-Bahman Blvd, Tabriz, East-Azarbiajan, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents the synthesis of blue-emissive nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDblue) via a rapid microwave-assisted pyrolysis method. To overcome the challenge of aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), which limits the application of quantum dots in solid-state devices, we propose embedding the N, S-GQDblue into polymer matrices. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) confirmed the presence of amine active sites, enabling crosslinking with epoxy monomers. Incorporating N, S-GQDblue into an epoxy matrix effectively mitigated ACQ effects while maintaining photoluminescence (PL) intensity, with over 86 % of PL retained after two months of storage. This study demonstrates potential applications in anti-forgery measures through stealth patterning and printing. Additionally, a Python-based image processing script was developed to authenticate printed patterns, achieving 100 % structural similarity and verifying emission wavelength accuracy. These findings advance the development of luminescent labels as foundational security elements, with promising applications in anti-counterfeiting, security devices, and optical sensing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Today, nanoparticles and nanocomposites are instrumental in enhancing various aspects of human life. They find applications in areas such as electrochemical hydrogen storage [1], electrochemical sensors [2], anticancer therapies [3], and photocatalysis [4], among others.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are emerging as promising zero-dimensional fluorescent materials, characterized by a transverse size below 100 nm. Chemically, they consist of stacked, functionalized graphene sheets, with an average thickness rarely exceeding 10 nm [5,6]. In recent years, GQDs have garnered significant attention due to their exceptional properties, such as low toxicity [7], biocompatibility [8], tunable luminescence [9], good water solubility [10], and photobleaching resistance [11]. These features make GQDs attractive alternatives to traditional fluorescent materials, particularly in applications like biosensors [12], sensing [13], bioimaging [14,15], and photocatalysis [16,17]. Despite their advantages, the broader application of GQDs has been limited by their low photoluminescence quantum yield [18,19]. Although several mechanisms, including zigzag edge states, quantum confinement effects, and surface functional groups, have been proposed to explain the photoluminescence properties of GQDs, the precise origin of their photoluminescence remains unclear [20]. Recent studies indicate that enhancing the photoluminescence properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be achieved through surface passivation or doping with heteroatoms and capping agents. Commonly reported heteroatoms used for this purpose include nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, and boron [21,22].

It is widely accepted that the incorporation of heteroatoms into graphene basal sheets induces surface defects, which alter the electron density distribution and subsequently increase the quantum yield by minimizing non-radiative recombination pathways [23,24]. Among the various synthesis methods, the ‘bottom-up’ approach—where graphene quantum dots are synthesized by carbonizing organic precursors—is favored over the ‘top-down’ method, which typically involves the wet chemical cutting of carbon precursors. The ‘bottom-up’ approach offers better control over morphology and size distribution, while also eliminating the need for complex equipment and secondary neutralization processes [25–29]. Compared to other convection-based thermal methods, such as hydrothermal treatments, microwave-assisted synthesis of graphene quantum dots has demonstrated superior performance, primarily due to its ability to significantly reduce reaction times [30,31]. This advantage is frequently noted in the context of more efficient synthesis [32,33].

However, one of the most critical challenges facing the application of carbon-based quantum dots in solid-state devices—such as light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and solar concentrators—is aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), which impairs their efficiency [34–37].To address this issue, embedding quantum dots in polymer matrices has been reported as an effective strategy to minimize ACQ. Notably, carbon-based quantum dots have been successfully passivated by incorporating them into polymeric structures such as siloxane or epoxy [38,39].

Counterfeiting presents a significant global challenge, leading to substantial economic losses and undermining social security. The rise in international trade and the importance of maintaining social security have underscored the urgent need for cost-effective advancements in anti-counterfeiting technologies. These technologies include holograms, metal threads, nuclear track technology, and luminescent labels, which have become crucial in combating counterfeiting and preserving the integrity of global trade systems while safeguarding social welfare[40–43]. Luminescent patterns, in particular, are highly valued for their unique optical properties under ultraviolet light, rapid response, and simplified verification processes, making them some of the most popular and effective deterrents against counterfeiting [21,44–46].

Nowadays, epoxy resins are some of the most important engineering polymers, due to their dimensional stability, chemical stability, and thermal-electrical insulation[47–51]. epoxy resins are used in a range of applications including coating [52–54], electric device insulation [55,56], printing luminescent labels [38,57]. It has been demonstrated that fluorescent epoxy nanocomposites are highly effective for anti-counterfeit pattern printing [38,58]. The excellent adhesion, and curing temperature below 100 °C of the QDs/epoxy resin mixture facilitate easy printing and patterning on diverse substrates, particularly cellulosic ones, without causing thermal degradation [38]. However, challenges such as reduced fluorescence intensity due to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) [41], changes in optical properties from environmental disturbances, and loss of epoxy resin transparency [59] need to be addressed.

In this study, we synthesized luminescent nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQD) via a microwave-assisted method, employing citric acid (CA), triethylenetetramine (TETA), and potassium persulfate (K2S2O8) as raw materials. The synergistic utilization of sulfur and nitrogen heteroatoms enabled the successful synthesis of luminescent graphene quantum dots. As anticipated, the synthesized N, S-GQDblue exhibits a notably high photoluminescence quantum yield of 43.18 %. Notably, we explore the potential of N, S-GQDblue as a complementary curing agent for epoxy monomers, a novel aspect addressed in this work. It is noteworthy that triethylenetetramine facilitates the formation of amine groups on the surface of N, S-GQDblue. These surface amine groups play a crucial role in expediting the curing process of epoxy monomers. Moreover, owing to the low viscosity of N, S-GQDblue/epoxy resin, cellulosic fibers and filaments can be easily impregnated without requiring high temperatures or pressure. We further demonstrate that the growth of polymeric chains on the surfaces of N, S-GQDblue leads to enhanced passivation, serving as a barrier against aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) phenomena. Additionally, leveraging a Python-based Advanced Image Processing Techniques, we subjected the printed patterns to rigorous validation procedures encompassing color and shape analyses. Through this meticulous validation process, we achieved a precision rate of 100.0 % in authenticating the congruence of the printed patterns with the designated control image. This research stands out favorably from analogous studies due to the following distinct attributes: 1) Utilization of cost-effective raw materials and a streamlined synthesis technique for the preparation of graphene dots. 2) Integration of Python-based coding facilitates the automated evaluation of the resemblance between printed patterns and the reference image, thereby enabling alongside automatic validation.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA) (99.99 %), Triethylenetetramine (TETA) (97.0 %), Ethylenediamine (EDA) (99.0 %), and Potassium persulfate (99.99 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. A low-viscosity bisphenol A-type epoxy monomer, Epiran-06(SPL), was obtained from Khouzestan Petrochemical, Iran.

A microwave-assisted pyrolysis method was employed to synthesize blue emissive N, SGQD. In a typical synthesize procedure, 0.40 mmol CA, 0.30 mmol TETA, and 0.30 mmol Persulfate salt were dissolved in 4 mL ultra-pure water. Subsequently, the transparent solution was heated in a domestic microwave oven (power 900 W) for 5–6 min, during which the color changed to dark brown. The obtained brown solid was dissolved in deionized water. Then the solution was dialyzed (3.5 kDa molecular weight cut-off) against deionized water for 24h. Finally, the dialyzed solution was freeze-dried and reserved. It was denoted as N, SGQD blue.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of pristine epoxy resins and N, S-GQDblue@ epoxy nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation pristine epoxy resin:

A pure epoxy resin was prepared by mixing 1.5 g of epoxy monomer with 0.75 ml of ethylenediamine and homogenizing the mixture at room temperature. The resulting mixture was then poured into a silicone mold with specific dimensions and cured at room temperature for 5 h.
Preparation of N, S-GQDblue@ epoxy nanocomposite:

A solution containing 50 mg of N, S-GQDblue in 4 ml of ethanol was prepared. This solution was added to the mixture of epoxy monomer and hardener, and after homogenization, it was used to create desired patterns on various substrates. The created pattern was allowed to dry naturally at room temperature.

Microwave synthesis of high luminescent N, S-GQDblue was carried out by reacting citric acid, Triethylenediamine, and Potassium persulfate in an aqueous solution. A schematic representation of the N, S-GQDblue synthesis route is shown in Fig. 1 .

The structural properties of N, S-GQDblue was studied by the XRD technique (Fig. S1). The X-ray diffraction pattern of N, S-GQDblue displays a broad peak at 2ϴ = 24.0° which corresponds to the d-spacing of 0.37 nm along the (002) direction which is 0.4 Å larger than that of pristine graphite, graphene basal planes functionalization can explain this difference. The formation of a crystal lattice resembling graphite is further supported by the observation of a barely perceptible shoulder at 2ϴ = 44°, which most likely corresponds to the (100) fringe of the graphite lattice.

Raman spectrum was recorded to identify the crystallinity of the synthesized nanostructure (Fig. 2 ). According to the group theory, the phonon branch of the graphene system is defined as Γgraphene = E2g + A1g + B1 [60]. It is typically observed that graphene-based nanostructures exhibit two specific peaks in their Raman spectrum around 1320 and 1580 cm−1. The Raman spectrum obtained from N, S-GQDblue has also an analogous nature. The A1g breathing mode is associated with sp3 or amorphous carbon, which is led to the D band centered at 1320 cm−1. The peak centered at 1580 cm−1 is caused by sp2 or graphitic carbon, which is known as the G band and attributed to E2g phonon mode. The defect intensity (ID/IG) of N, S-GQDblue was found to be 1.05, which is larger than this ratio in non-doped graphene quantum dots (1.01) [61]. In light of the fact that defect intensity correlates with the amount of structural defects, it can be concluded that by doping, the edge roughness and defects of graphene sheets are intensified, thereby ID/IG ratio was increased.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images of the synthesized graphene quantum dots were captured to evaluate their particle size distribution and to visualize the lattice structures (Fig. 3 a). displays the TEM image of a portion of purified N, S-GQDblue that was separated from the bulk by sonication. The Gaussian fitting size distribution curves (Fig. 3b) corresponding to the TEM image displays that the size of stacked graphene sheets ranges from 4 to 16 nm, with an average size of 8.63 nm. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images clearly display the high-contrast lattice fringes with spacings of 0.21 and 0.37 nm, associated with in-plane lattice spacing (100) and basal plane spacing (002) [62] of graphite-like structure, respectively (Fig. 3c and d). Furthermore, a crossed lattice pattern with a lattice spacing of 0.248 nm, was observed which corresponds to the (1120) lattice fringes of graphene, confirming that some of the dots are individual graphenes[63–65]. In Fig. 3e, the crystallinity of the structure of the carbon dots is confirmed by the FFT image corresponding to Fig. 3d. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of N, S-GQDblue is illustrated in Fig. 3f, where a large number of randomly oriented nano-graphenes have left concentric diffraction rings with few bright spots. The indexing of the diffraction rings revealed a diffuse polycrystalline pattern of the graphitic structure of N, S-GQDs with the main planes identifies in (004), (100), and (1120).

To examine and compare the morphology of pristine epoxy resin and N, S-GQDblue@ epoxy nanocomposite, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained for both materials. As shown in Fig. S2, the N, S-GQDblue@ epoxy nanocomposite exhibit significant surface roughness attributed to the incorporation of graphene quantum dots, which promote the heterogeneous growth of epoxy chains on nanoparticle surfaces. In contrast, the pristine epoxy resin displays a polished, smooth, and laminated surface morphology.

The surface functional groups of the N, S-GQDblue was investigated using XPS (Fig. 4 a). Based on the XPS spectrum, it was confirmed that N, S-GQDblue is composed from nitrogen, sulfur, carbon, and oxygen elements, which each of these elements has a relative content of 13.21, 0.77, 63.28, and 22.73. Deconvoluting high-resolution XP C1s, N1s, and S2p spectra provided more detailed information about surface functional groups. Deconvoluting high-resolution XP C1s is demonstrated in (Fig. 4b) to contain several C-binding sites at 284.6, 285.9, 287.5, 284.6 eV, representing CC, C–O/N, CO, C–C/S/H, respectively. As illustrated in (inset of Fig. 4b). the graphitization of carbon precursor, Citric Acid, is confirmed by the fact that over 30 % of the carbon content is related to CC.

C–O/N functional groups accounted for 40 % of the total carbon content, which is consistent with the total oxygen and nitrogen content in XPS. Upon deconvolution XP N1s, it was found that the nitrogen content of N, S-GQDblue included amino/pyrrolic and pyridinic nitrogen with bond energies of 400.05, 398.7 eV, respectively (Fig. 4c). As shown in (inset of Fig. 4c) approximately 35 % of the nitrogen in N, S-GQDblue is attributed to N-Amino/pyrrolic, which are hydrogen-terminated and can participate in epoxy curing.

Fig. 4d illustrates the deconvolution of a high-resolution XPS short scan of S2p that indicates two S-binding sites at 166.75 eV (C–S) and 168.07 eV (Sulphonate). The relative content of these functional groups is displayed in (inset of Fig. 4d)

To evaluate whether the synthesized N, S-GQDblue could participate in epoxy curing, a liquid epoxy resin based on diglycidyl ether bisphenol-A with low viscosity was chosen. At a temperature of 250C, the ethanol solution containing purified N, S-GQDblue was added to a mixture of liquid epoxy resin and Ethylenediamine that had been prepared in advance and finally epoxy monomer was cured at room temperature. Consistent with XPS analysis in Fig. 4c, the hydrogen-terminated nitrogen atoms on the N, S-GQDblue surface, are illustrated to be available for epoxy monomer curing (Fig. 5 ). An analysis of FT-IR spectroscopy can provide insight into the possibility of embedding graphene quantum dots in epoxy resin networks during the curing process.

First, the functional groups of the N, S-GQDblue was investigated, as illustrated in Fig. 6 . In the FT-IR spectrum of N, S-GQDblue, absorption bands related to C–N, SO, conjugated CO, aromatic CC, and O–H were observed at 1082, 1410, 1710, 1560& 1650, and 2700-3200 cm−1, respectively.

It is worth noting that the relatively strong broad absorption band at 3300-3500 cm−1 can be attributed to the N–H stretching vibration of amine, which is consistent with XPS results. In Fig. 6, the FT-IR spectra of epoxy resin and N, S-GQDblue@ epoxy nanocomposite can be seen. Both epoxy resin and N, S-GQDblue@ epoxy nanocomposite exhibited completely identical absorption bands at 1511& 1611 and 2800-3000 cm−1 which corresponded to CC& C–C, and aliphatic and aromatic C–H stretching, respectively. However, the difference in intensity of absorption bands related to C–O–C symmetric stretching& symmetric ring breathing caused by the oxirane ring at 830& 1230 cm−1 provides important information regarding the Chemical embedding capability of N, S-GQDblue. The obvious decrease in intensity of these peaks supports the conclusion that hydrogen-terminated nitrogen atoms located on N, S-GQDblue have participated in curing epoxy monomers, and the chemical embedding of quantum dots has been successful. Additionally, the presence of Free O–H at 3850 cm−1, adjacent to H-Binding O–H at 3600 cm−1, is further evidence for graphene quantum dots' involvement in epoxy monomer curing. The very large rotational energy barrier of the C–N bond in epoxy resin due to the involvement of heavy graphite particles in monomer curing prevents hydrogen bond from being formed [59,66].

Fig. 7 a demonstrates the optical characterization results of N, S-GQDblue. The purified N, S-GQDblue displays an intense absorption band at 350 nm, which is commonly attributed to the n→π∗ transition of conjugated CO, and CN [67]. The 250 nm strong absorption peak, caused by π→π∗ transitions of aromatic CC, was also observed [67]. Quantum yield (QY) of N, SCQDblue in comparison to the Quinine sulfate (QS) as the reference dye, based on the following formula was investigated (Eq. (1)). (QY of QS in 0.1 M H2SO4 (ƞ = 1.33) has been reported to be 0.547).
(Eq. 1)
Φ _
( s )
= Φ _
( r )
×
(
I
s
I r
)
×
(
A
r
A s
)
×
(
ƞ s
ƞ r
)
2
Where, Φ, I, A, and η are assigned to QY, integrated PL intensity, absorbance values, and refractive index, respectively. The subscript r and s refer reference fluorophore of known QY and sample, respectively. Based on the results of the calculations, it was determined that the photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) surpasses the threshold of 40 %. The light emitted from an aqueous solution of N, SGQDblue by varying the excitation wavelength was recorded using photoluminescence spectroscopy. As can be inferred from Fig. 7b, as the excitation wavelength increases, the emission intensity increases along with it. Even so, the maximum emission wavelength and FWHM of the emission peaks remain constant. It worth to note, a negligible red-shift (3 nm) along with a slight reduction in emissions intensity are observed with an increase in excitation wavelength from 360 nm to 380 nm, contrary to the expected trend. In line with previous studies on graphene quantum dots, the N, S-GQDblue dots demonstrate excitation-independent photoluminescence. The phenomenon is commonly ascribed to passive surface traps introduced by amine functional groups [68].

In view of N, S-codoped graphene quantum dot's non-uniform size distribution and its excitation-independent PL, it can be said that surface defect states are primarily responsible for their photoluminescence. It is believed that the fluorescence of carbon-based quantum dots is caused by surface defect states originated from heteroatoms (including O, N, S) capturing excitons. A new type of surface state called N-State enters the vicinity of the O-State due to graphene framework doping with nitrogen and extends further away from the carbonic core. As illustrated in (Fig. S3), as one moves away from the carbon core, the density of each surface state's constituent energy levels increases. It's important to note that the distribution of energy levels within nonradiative levels is the opposite of the trend that takes place in surface states. The incorporation of the sulfur heteroatom into the structure of graphene quantum dots by re-expanding the N-State results in an increase in the density of its energy levels away from the carbonic core. Hence, the extending of the N-state will be able to provide a high radiative recombination efficiency by limiting the non-radiative levels susceptible to non-radiative transitions. It may be due to being close in energy levels in the Expanded N-state that the synthesized graphene quantum dots emit blue light excitation-independent. Because based on surface state defect theory, Luminescence properties in synthesized N, S-GQDblue are thought to be produced by radiative recombination between expanded N-state and ground state.

In order for epoxy nanocomposite to be used in printing anti-counterfeit patterns, its long-term PL stability is of vital importance. Therefore, in order to determine whether the N, S-GQDblue@ epoxy nanocomposite in question has permanent photoluminescence properties, we investigated its PL stability under natural conditions. As shown in Fig. 8 the epoxy nanocomposite retained 86.6 % of its initial PL intensity despite being stored in ambient conditions for 8 weeks. In comparison to the findings reported by Lee et al. [38], the observed six percent enhancement in initial photoluminescence retention over the same time frame demonstrates the superior environmental stability of the synthesized graphene quantum dots. This suggests their improved resistance to external disturbances, highlighting their potential for robust applications. Contrary to this, the aqueous solution of N, S-GQDblue no longer retains its luminescent properties after being under identical conditions and duration of storage. Concerning the storage stability of N, S-GQDblue in dried form under ambient conditions, it is notable that the QY showed only a slight reduction of 2.36 %, stabilizing at 40.82 % after more than a year. For further characterization, the dynamic light scattering (DLS) size distribution of N, S-GQDblue dispersed in water and an SEM image of N, S-GQDblue are provided in Fig. S4. These analyses indicate that, even after prolonged storage, N, S-GQDblue maintain their core properties with minimal structural changes.

By observing these results, it can be concluded that the direct growth of epoxy chains on N, S-GQDblue caused their passivation during chemical embedding. Considering suitable viscosity (292.0 cP) and room-temperature cures, N, S-GQDblue/epoxy resin mixtures could be easily patterned on a range of cellulose and non-cellulose substrates (e.g., paper, leather). It is believed that hydrogen-terminated nitrogen found in the edges of N, S-GQDblue could accelerate the curing process at room temperature without any external heat being applied. As far as the security level of these luminescent labels is concerned, reverse engineering of these labels is impossible since N, S-GQDblue, epoxy resin matrix, and the aforementioned substrates have identical elements. Therefore, epoxy monomer curing can be facilitated at room temperature by adding N, S-GQDblue, enabling covert printing using epoxy/ethylenediamine/N, S-GQDblue combination. Fig. 9 illustrates how epoxy/EDA/N, S-GQDblue combination can be used to print and stamp the pattern on leather without heat treatment. Fig. S5 presents the PL spectrum of the N, S-GQDblue@ epoxy nanocomposite, recorded at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. It worth to note, after preparation, the QY of the luminescent labels was determined to be 29.15 %.

Fig. S6 displays the XRD spectrum of the N, S-GQDblue@ epoxy nanocomposite. Even after more than a year of storage under ambient conditions, this spectrum shows minimal deviation from the XRD spectrum of the epoxy resin reported in previous studies [69], suggesting that the structural integrity of N, S-GQDblue@ epoxy nanocomposite is well-maintained over time.

Ultimately, employing Python programming, the mean color of the printed pattern and the resemblance between the luminescent label and the reference image were assessed via the ORB algorithm. Subsequently, verification ensued by adhering to predetermined similarity thresholds and circular mean color ranges. Fig. 10 demonstrates that the pattern imprinted on the paper substrate underwent digital processing, revealing a high congruence of 100.0 % with the reference image.

In our research, we utilized advanced AI programming techniques for image processing and analysis, which significantly enhanced the rigor and reliability of our findings. The following detailed protocols outline the methodologies implemented in our study:
1.
Background Removal: To isolate the subject from its background, we employed a background removal function. This process begins with converting the input image to grayscale using OpenCV's cvtColor function. We then applied a binary thresholding technique, where a threshold value of 240 was utilized to create a mask that distinguishes the subject from the background. The mask was inverted to effectively highlight the subject. Finally, we applied this mask to the original image using the bitwise_and function, which allowed for a clean separation of the subject for further analysis.
2.
Subject Color Analysis: To assess the color characteristics of the isolated subject, we converted the image to the HSV (Hue, Saturation, Value) color space. The average HSV values were computed using a masked region derived from the background removal step. This analysis provides insights into the color properties of the subject, which are crucial for the validation of our research criteria.
3.
ORB Feature Matching: We implemented the ORB (Oriented FAST and Rotated BRIEF) feature matching algorithm to quantitatively compare the isolated subject with a reference image. This involved detecting key points and computing their descriptors using OpenCV's ORB_create function. We employed a brute-force matcher to identify and compare the descriptors, allowing us to assess the similarity between the two images. A similarity threshold was set, whereby regions with a distance of less than 75 between matched key points were considered similar. This quantitative measure is vital for determining the authenticity of the subject image.
4.
Mean Hue Calculation: To further refine our color analysis, we calculated the circular mean of the hue values extracted from the subject image. This was achieved by first flattening the hue channel and converting the values into radians to accommodate the circular nature of hue. The circular mean was then computed using the arctan2 function, ensuring a robust representation of the subject's predominant hue.

The integration of these AI programming protocols allowed us to streamline our analysis, ensuring accuracy and reliability in our results. By leveraging these methodologies, we were able to effectively validate our findings, demonstrating the significance of employing AI techniques in our research.
Table 1 presents a comparison between the current study and a related study in the field of luminescent labels.

A microwave-assisted thermal pyrolysis process was used to synthesize N, S-GQDblue with high PLQY. The successful incorporation of nitrogen and sulfur into the graphene framework was resulted in the creation of an Expanded N-State which consequently led to a quantum yield of 43.18 %. In order to overcome the destructive and inevitable ACQ process, the chemical embedding of N, S-GQDblue in an epoxy matrix was accomplished successfully due to the presence of amine active sites at the edges of these compounds. Besides accelerating the curing process at ambient temperatures, growing the epoxy chains on the surface of N, S-GQDblue resulted in better passivation of quantum dots. The photoluminescence intensity of N, S-GQDblue@ epoxy nanocomposite is lost less than 15 % within 8 weeks of being stored at ambient temperatures. The homogeneity, transparency, and commonality among most of the constituent elements of the epoxy/EDA/N, S-GQDblue mixture and the printing substrate are other advantages that highlight the abilities of N, S-GQDblue as a luminescent agent. Ultimately, the integration of a digital processing algorithm enables the systematic and quantitative evaluation of the congruence between the printed labels and the reference image, thereby enhancing the precision and dependability of the analysis. The attainment of a stellar validation score of 100.0 % in our investigation furnishes compelling substantiation that our materials and methodologies represent a viable and auspicious choice for real-world implementations.
Vahid Ashrafi Lighvan: Writing – original draft, Software, Formal analysis. Nasser Arsalani: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2024.130192.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Li, S. Zhang, S.A. Kulinich, Y. Liu, H. Zeng, Engineering surface states of carbon dots to achieve controllable luminescence for solid-luminescent composites and sensitive Be2+ detection, Sci. Rep. 4 (2014) 4976.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A comparative study on structural, morphological, and tensile properties of binary and ternary epoxy resin‐based polymer nanocomposites
Adv. Mater. Sci. Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.M.T. Mustafa, S. Rostam, S.B. Aziz, A Comparative Study on Structural, Morphological, and Tensile Properties of Binary and Ternary Epoxy Resin-Based Polymer Nanocomposites, Adv. Mater. Sci. Eng. 2020 (2020) 7914796.
